A business development operator in the blockchain industry

BD 2026 Guidebook

A practical operating manual for business developers in web3, with a global view and a Korea-led edge.

March 12, 2026

Chapter 01

Cover

How to use this guide and what changed in 2025-2026.

BD 2026 Guidebook

Who this is for

This book is for a business developer working in web3, blockchain, or digital assets who wants to operate with sharper commercial judgment in 2026.

It is written for someone who needs both:

  • a global view of where real demand, policy support, and buyer budgets are moving
  • a Korea-led perspective that can turn local market understanding into a differentiated edge

The short thesis

The center of gravity in web3 business development has shifted.

The highest-value work is no longer "find hype, launch token, get listings."

The highest-value work is now:

  • move money better
  • tokenize assets with legal wrappers
  • help institutions and enterprises enter the market safely
  • sell compliance, custody, and operating reliability as part of the product
  • build distribution around regulated use cases, not just protocol narratives

What changed in 2025-2026

Several things moved from narrative to operating reality:

  • Stablecoins became a mainstream product discussion across payments, treasury, settlement, and B2B cross-border workflows.
  • Tokenization moved from theory toward actual regulated issuance infrastructure and distribution.
  • Regulation became clearer in major markets, which means BD now needs to understand legal perimeter, bankability, and licensing constraints.
  • Korea became more interesting not because of retail speculation alone, but because policy, banking, and enterprise experimentation started to matter more.

How to use this guide

Read the chapters in order once.

Then use the book in three passes:

  1. Market pass: understand where value is forming and which BMs deserve your time.
  2. Operator pass: learn how to run pipeline, partner discovery, and account strategy in a way that closes real business.
  3. Career pass: package your knowledge into a differentiated profile that compounds over time.

What this book is trying to help you do

By the end, you should be able to:

  • explain the hottest web3 BMs in plain business terms
  • identify which opportunities are durable versus narrative-only
  • speak credibly with fintechs, exchanges, banks, enterprises, and regulators
  • build a 30-60-90 day BD plan
  • create your own view on what Korea can export into the global market

A note on "hot"

"Hot" does not mean noisy.

In this book, "hot" means one or more of:

  • budgets are opening
  • regulation is clarifying
  • enterprise buyers are active
  • take rates can be explained
  • the product has repeatable operational value
  • the category is likely to still matter in 24 months

Chapter 02

Global Market Map

Where the real money, urgency, and buyer budgets are forming.

Global Market Map

The current value map

flowchart LR
    BD[2026 BD\nPriority Lanes]
    BD --> L1[Lane 1\nStablecoin\nPayments]
    BD --> L2[Lane 2\nTokenization\n& RWA]
    BD --> L3[Lane 3\nCompliance\n& Custody]
    BD --> L4[Lane 4\nData &\nAnalytics]
    BD --> L5[Lane 5\nSpecialized\nInfra]

A useful BD lens is simple:

  • Where is money moving?
  • Where are regulated buyers appearing?
  • Where does distribution already exist?
  • Where can a team defend margin?

In 2026, the best answers are concentrated in a few lanes.

Lane 1: Stablecoin payments and settlement

This is the most commercially proven lane in web3 today.

Why it matters:

  • It solves a painful problem with visible economic value.
  • Buyers already understand payments, treasury, FX, and settlement.
  • It fits existing enterprise and fintech budgets.
  • It connects crypto rails to non-crypto workflows.

What products sit here:

  • cross-border B2B payouts
  • merchant settlement
  • contractor and creator payouts
  • treasury management
  • cards and card settlement
  • on-ramp and off-ramp infrastructure
  • liquidity routing and compliance tooling

Why this lane is durable:

  • It benefits from network effects and distribution.
  • Margins are not purely speculative; they come from fees, spreads, float, and workflow efficiency.
  • It has enterprise expansion paths into reconciliation, reporting, treasury, and compliance.

What a BD should know:

  • The economics of pre-funding versus just-in-time settlement
  • The difference between a stablecoin issuer, payment orchestrator, and wallet infra provider
  • The role of banking partners and fiat corridors
  • Compliance constraints by jurisdiction

Lane 2: Tokenization and RWA infrastructure

Tokenization is becoming real where it reduces distribution friction, operational cost, or market fragmentation.

The strongest subcategories:

  • tokenized treasuries
  • tokenized funds
  • private credit rails
  • security token infrastructure
  • tokenized receivables and invoice flows
  • digital transfer-agent style systems

What the market actually pays for:

  • issuance workflow
  • investor onboarding
  • transfer restrictions
  • custody and cap-table logic
  • reporting and servicing
  • secondary trading connectivity

What weak teams get wrong:

  • they pitch "tokenization" as a feature without proving who gains distribution, liquidity, or operational efficiency
  • they ignore legal wrapper and compliance cost
  • they assume any asset can be made liquid just by putting it onchain

Lane 3: Compliance, custody, and enterprise access

This is less glamorous and extremely important.

When institutions enter the market, they need a stack:

  • custody
  • policy controls
  • wallet governance
  • transaction monitoring
  • travel rule support
  • proof of reserves or proof of controls
  • accounting and reporting
  • fiat movement and banking coordination

The BD opportunity:

  • Many customers do not want "crypto products."
  • They want safe access, internal controls, and low-risk workflows.
  • Selling trust, controls, and process maturity often closes faster than selling ideology.

Lane 4: Data, analytics, and risk infrastructure

Data becomes valuable when it changes a decision.

Useful data businesses in web3 include:

  • compliance and screening data
  • wallet intelligence
  • market surveillance
  • treasury and balance sheet analytics
  • validator and staking risk analytics
  • token distribution intelligence

The trap:

  • generic dashboards are easy to copy
  • a data business needs workflow insertion, proprietary data, or compliance relevance

Lane 5: Specialized infrastructure with attached distribution

Infrastructure still matters, but raw infrastructure without distribution is difficult.

Examples that can work:

  • MPC wallet infrastructure with enterprise clients
  • intent and routing layers embedded inside applications
  • gaming, creator, or commerce rails with clear channel partners
  • validator, staking, or restaking services tied to institutional distribution

The key condition:

  • infrastructure wins when it is packaged into a business workflow, not when it is sold as technical elegance

What is losing relative attention

These areas are not necessarily dead, but they are weaker as default BD priorities:

  • generic L1 and L2 ecosystem BD without real application pull
  • NFT-only consumer models
  • exchange listing and token launch as the core business
  • retail-only speculative acquisition
  • protocol grants as a substitute for actual revenue

How to think globally

flowchart LR
    P["Policy Layer\nWhich jurisdictions\nare opening or tightening?"]
    B["Buyer Layer\nWhich buyer types are active?\nWho owns the budget?"]
    D["Distribution Layer\nWhich channels deliver\nrepeatable customers?"]
    P --> B --> D

A good global BD frame has three layers:

  • Policy layer: Which jurisdictions are opening or tightening?
  • Buyer layer: Which buyer types are active and what budget owner signs?
  • Distribution layer: Which channels can take your product to repeatable customers?

This matters more than following crypto social media narratives.

Where to spend your time

If you only had three domains to master deeply in 2026, choose:

  • stablecoin payments
  • tokenization and securities market structure
  • regulated enterprise onboarding into digital assets

Chapter 03

Business Models That Matter

The hottest and most durable BMs in web3 right now.

Business Models That Matter

How to evaluate a web3 BM

flowchart TD
    BM[Evaluate the Business Model]
    BM --> F1[Buyer urgency]
    BM --> F2[Willingness to pay]
    BM --> F3[Regulatory clarity]
    BM --> F4[Distribution difficulty]
    BM --> F5[Gross margin quality]
    BM --> F6[Durability after hype]
    F1 & F2 & F3 & F4 & F5 & F6 --> S{4 or more filters\npass strongly?}
    S -->|Yes| GO[Strong BM candidate]
    S -->|No| WAIT[Too early or too crowded]

Use six filters:

  • buyer urgency
  • willingness to pay
  • regulatory clarity
  • distribution difficulty
  • gross margin quality
  • durability after hype fades

The best 2026 models usually score well on at least four of the six.

1. Stablecoin payments infrastructure

Core customer

  • fintechs
  • payment service providers
  • exchanges
  • global payroll and payout platforms
  • marketplaces
  • exporters and importers

Revenue model

  • transaction fees
  • FX spread
  • treasury and float income
  • SaaS platform fees
  • premium compliance or routing fees

Why it works

  • The pain is concrete.
  • Savings can be measured against SWIFT, correspondent banking, and trapped working capital.
  • Customers already have payment budgets.

What creates moat

  • banking relationships
  • corridor liquidity
  • licensing position
  • compliance stack
  • enterprise integrations

Key KPIs

  • payment volume
  • active corridors
  • time to settlement
  • take rate by corridor
  • loss or incident rate
  • conversion from quote to funded flow

Korea angle

Strong for:

  • exporter settlement
  • creator and gaming payouts
  • overseas contractor payroll
  • treasury operations for companies with global vendors

2. RWA and tokenization rails

Core customer

  • asset managers
  • issuers
  • brokerages
  • securities firms
  • custodians
  • banks

Revenue model

  • issuance fees
  • admin and servicing fees
  • custody fees
  • transfer and compliance fees
  • marketplace transaction fees

Why it works

  • It converts complex, manual operations into programmable workflows.
  • It can lower minimum ticket size and widen distribution.
  • It creates recurring operational revenue after issuance.

What creates moat

  • legal and compliance design
  • issuer distribution
  • regulated market access
  • integration with custody and transfer restrictions

Key KPIs

  • assets issued
  • active investors
  • issuer retention
  • average servicing revenue per asset
  • time to onboard a new issuer

Korea angle

Strong for:

  • STO infrastructure
  • receivables and invoice-backed products
  • culture, media, and IP-linked structuring if legally sound
  • distribution partnerships with regulated financial players

3. Custody and governance stack

Core customer

  • enterprises
  • funds
  • trading firms
  • banks exploring digital asset operations
  • foundations and protocols with treasury controls

Revenue model

  • SaaS seat or wallet fees
  • assets under custody fees
  • policy engine upsells
  • implementation and support

Why it works

  • It addresses board-level risk concerns.
  • Procurement can be justified under security and control budgets.
  • It becomes sticky after policies and approvals are embedded.

Key moat

  • security reputation
  • regulatory posture
  • integrations with exchanges, banks, and workflows
  • audit readiness

4. Compliance and transaction intelligence

Core customer

  • exchanges
  • custodians
  • fintechs
  • banks
  • OTC desks

Revenue model

  • subscription
  • usage-based screening fees
  • enterprise contracts

Why it works

  • Mandatory spend is better than optional spend.
  • As regulation matures, compliance budgets grow faster than narrative budgets.

What creates moat

  • proprietary entity labels
  • investigator workflow
  • trust with regulators and compliance teams

5. Staking, validator, and yield infrastructure

Core customer

  • institutions
  • funds
  • wallets
  • protocols

Revenue model

  • staking commission
  • white-label infrastructure fees
  • reporting and institutional support fees

Why it works

  • There is recurring revenue and operational stickiness.
  • Institutions need reliable operators, not hobbyist infrastructure.

The caution

  • This market can commoditize.
  • Margin defense requires reliability, distribution, or premium service.

6. Developer tooling and infrastructure

When it works

  • when embedded in customer workflow
  • when linked to enterprise adoption
  • when sold with reliability or compliance outcomes

When it fails

  • when the tool is technically elegant but not required for budget approval
  • when the buyer is another startup with no durable spending power

Ranking the hottest BMs for 2026

graph TD
    subgraph Ranking["2026 BM Priority — Highest to Lowest"]
        R1["#1  Stablecoin Payments & Treasury Infra"]
        R2["#2  Tokenization, RWA & STO Rails"]
        R3["#3  Custody, Compliance & Enterprise Controls"]
        R4["#4  Institutional Staking & Validator Services"]
        R5["#5  Specialized Data & Intelligence Products"]
        R6["#6  Infrastructure with Distribution Attached"]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6
    end

If the question is commercial heat plus durability, the order is:

  1. Stablecoin payments and treasury infrastructure
  2. Tokenization, RWA operations, and STO rails
  3. Custody, compliance, and enterprise controls
  4. Institutional staking and validator services with distribution
  5. Specialized data and intelligence products
  6. Infrastructure that has distribution attached

Mental model for BD

A BM is good if you can answer three questions clearly:

  • Who pays?
  • Why now?
  • Why you instead of another provider or an internal build?

If those answers are vague, the category is probably still too early or too crowded.

Chapter 04

Regulation And Market Structure

What a BD must know to avoid wasting cycles and to win regulated deals.

Regulation And Market Structure

Why BD needs regulatory fluency

In web3, regulation is not a side topic.

It affects:

  • whether a deal can legally exist
  • who inside the customer can approve it
  • which banking partner will support it
  • how long procurement takes
  • whether revenue can scale across jurisdictions

BD without regulatory fluency creates pipeline that legal, compliance, or operations later kills.

Your minimum operating model

For every target market, track:

  • asset classification
  • licensing or registration perimeter
  • AML and travel rule obligations
  • stablecoin treatment
  • custody expectations
  • securities law exposure
  • tax and reporting friction

United States

The U.S. remains strategically important because global companies still benchmark against U.S. policy and capital markets.

What matters now:

  • stablecoin policy moved forward materially in 2025
  • institutional digital asset products gained more legitimacy
  • federal versus state perimeter still matters

Practical BD takeaway:

  • even if you do not sell in the U.S. first, you should understand U.S. policy because counterparties, investors, and partners will ask about it

European Union

MiCA matters because it gives a clearer operating framework than many markets.

Why this matters for BD:

  • a clearer framework reduces uncertainty in partner conversations
  • licensed entities become better distribution partners
  • compliance maturity becomes a selling point, not a drag

Practical BD takeaway:

  • if your company can work with MiCA-aligned institutions or practices, you gain credibility elsewhere too

Hong Kong and Singapore

These markets matter because they are Asian reference points for institutional digital asset business.

What they signal:

  • digital asset regulation in Asia is becoming more product-specific and infrastructure-aware
  • stablecoin, custody, and market conduct rules are central

Practical BD takeaway:

  • if you are Korea-based, follow Hong Kong and Singapore closely because regional buyers compare market readiness across these hubs

Korea

Korea deserves its own strategic lens.

What matters:

  • strong retail awareness already exists
  • banking relationships are central to what can scale
  • legal clarity is improving, but product design still needs care
  • institutional and corporate participation matters more than before

Key business implication:

  • in Korea, distribution, trust, and bank-compatible structure can matter more than pure product speed

Market structure knowledge every BD should build

You should understand the role of:

  • issuers
  • custodians
  • banks
  • broker-dealers and securities firms
  • exchanges and OTC desks
  • market makers
  • wallet infrastructure providers
  • compliance vendors
  • payment service providers

If you cannot map those players, you will not see where your product actually fits.

Jurisdiction snapshot

graph LR
    subgraph US["United States"]
        US1[Stablecoin policy\nadvanced in 2025]
        US2[Federal vs state\nperimeter still matters]
    end
    subgraph EU["European Union"]
        EU1[MiCA framework\nin effect]
        EU2[Licensed entities =\nbetter partners]
    end
    subgraph APAC["HK & Singapore"]
        AP1[Stablecoin & custody\nrules maturing]
        AP2[Asian reference\npoints for institutions]
    end
    subgraph KR["Korea"]
        KR1[Banking relationships\nare the practical gate]
        KR2[Institutional participation\ngrowing]
    end

The five regulatory questions to ask before serious BD

flowchart TD
    Q1[Is the asset or workflow\nlegal in this market?]
    Q2[Does the customer need\na license or regulated partner?]
    Q3[Who is the custodian,\nissuer, or settlement principal?]
    Q4[What AML, KYC, travel rule,\nor reporting requirements attach?]
    Q5[Which part breaks\nif the bank says no?]
    Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4 --> Q5 --> OK[Safe to proceed\nwith deal design]
  1. Is the asset or workflow clearly legal in the target market?
  2. Does the customer need a license or regulated partner to use the product?
  3. Which entity is the merchant of record, custodian, issuer, or settlement principal?
  4. What AML, KYC, travel rule, or reporting requirements attach?
  5. Which part of the design breaks if the bank says no?

How regulation changes messaging

Weak BD messaging:

  • faster
  • decentralized
  • onchain
  • innovative

Better BD messaging:

  • lower settlement friction
  • clearer controls
  • auditable workflow
  • lower operational risk
  • better working capital efficiency
  • compliance-ready expansion

Working rule

Never pitch a regulated buyer with a crypto-native vocabulary first.

Start with business problem, controls, and economics.

Only then explain the blockchain component.

Chapter 05

Korea-Led Strategy

How to think from Korea outward instead of copying U.S. narratives.

Korea-Led Strategy

Start from Korea's real advantages

Do not build your view of Korea around retail speculation alone.

Korea has stronger strategic value when you look at:

  • globally connected exporters and service firms
  • gaming and digital content ecosystems
  • dense fintech and banking infrastructure
  • sophisticated retail and mobile payments behavior
  • policy evolution around digital assets and tokenization

The Korea-led thesis

The strongest Korea-led web3 BD strategy is not "copy a U.S. token narrative and localize it."

It is:

  • identify Korean industries with cross-border money flow
  • use stablecoin and digital asset rails where they reduce friction
  • attach regulated counterparties early
  • build enterprise-grade trust and operations
  • export the model to regional or global partners

Best Korean opportunity areas

1. Cross-border B2B settlement

Why it fits Korea:

  • export-heavy economy
  • many firms deal with overseas suppliers, agencies, and contractors
  • treasury efficiency and settlement speed are understandable value propositions

Target buyers:

  • mid-market exporters
  • trading firms
  • agencies with international contractor networks
  • commerce aggregators

2. Gaming, creator, and digital content payouts

Why it fits Korea:

  • Korea already produces globally distributed digital content
  • payout fragmentation is painful across countries and intermediaries
  • web3 rails can be sold as payout infrastructure rather than as "crypto"

Target buyers:

  • game studios
  • creator networks
  • music and content agencies
  • fan platform operators

3. STO and tokenized financial products

Why it fits Korea:

  • there is institutional familiarity with structured finance
  • financial incumbents matter
  • legal foundation is becoming more important than pure experimentation

Target buyers:

  • securities firms
  • asset managers
  • infrastructure providers to regulated issuers

4. Enterprise digital asset access stack

Why it fits Korea:

  • enterprises entering digital assets will want controlled exposure
  • they will need custody, approvals, reporting, and policy support
  • local trust and relationship management matter

Target buyers:

  • corporates
  • funds
  • banks exploring infrastructure partnerships

Korea-specific BD lessons

Banks are not optional

In Korea, banking relationships are often the practical gate.

A solution that sounds good but cannot survive banking scrutiny is weaker than it looks.

Enterprise trust matters more than crypto branding

For many Korean buyers, especially larger ones, "crypto-native" branding can raise procurement friction.

Translate the value into:

  • settlement
  • controls
  • treasury
  • digital asset operations
  • infrastructure modernization

Local references are powerful

A Korean buyer often cares about:

  • who else in Korea is already using a similar workflow
  • which law firm, bank, or compliance advisor is involved
  • whether regulators and banking partners will understand the structure

Distribution beats abstract strategy

Useful channels in Korea can include:

  • banks
  • securities firms
  • fintechs
  • enterprise software vendors
  • large agency networks
  • payment and commerce ecosystems

Your Korea-outward expansion playbook

flowchart LR
    A["1. Validate\nKorean use case\nwith real buyer pain"]
    B["2. Add credibility\nanchors\nlegal, bank, partner"]
    C["3. Build operational\nworkflow\nnot just a pilot"]
    D["4. Export the\nKorean case study"]
    E["5. Japan · HK\nSingapore · Global"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E

Use this order:

  1. Validate the use case with a Korean buyer segment that already feels the pain.
  2. Add one or two credibility anchors such as legal counsel, banking dialogue, or a regulated partner.
  3. Build the operational workflow, not just the pilot.
  4. Use the Korean case study to sell into Japan, Hong Kong, Singapore, or global counterparties with similar needs.

Where Korean operators can win globally

  • high-touch enterprise sales
  • operational reliability
  • regional relationship building
  • fintech and payment partnerships
  • structured, compliance-aware tokenization products

A warning

Korea can also trap you into a domestic-only mindset.

Do not confuse:

  • local noise with exportable advantage
  • retail attention with enterprise demand
  • partnership announcements with real distribution

Chapter 06

BD Operator Manual

How to run pipeline, partnerships, discovery, and close motion.

BD Operator Manual

What good BD actually is

Good BD is not just networking.

It is a disciplined system for:

  • selecting the right market
  • prioritizing accounts
  • discovering real pain
  • converting interest into an executable commercial motion
  • coordinating legal, product, operations, and management to close

The BD stack

graph TD
    BD[BD Operating Stack]
    BD --> M["1. Market Thesis\nWho, what, why now — one page"]
    BD --> A["2. Account Selection\nAttributes over social graph"]
    BD --> D["3. Discovery\nSurface pain, not present product"]
    BD --> DA["4. Deal Architecture\nLegal, custody, metrics, pilot scope"]
    BD --> PC["5. Post-Close Expansion\nSecond workflow, platform dependency"]

You need five layers:

  • market thesis
  • account selection
  • discovery
  • deal architecture
  • post-close expansion

1. Market thesis

Your thesis should fit on one page.

It must answer:

  • which customer type has the pain
  • which workflow you improve
  • which buyer owns the budget
  • why your timing is good now
  • what makes your approach hard to replace

If your thesis is vague, your pipeline will be vague.

2. Account selection

Build your target list by attributes, not by social graph.

Useful account filters:

  • cross-border activity
  • digital asset readiness
  • regulated footprint
  • partnership velocity
  • existing treasury or settlement pain
  • history of fintech integrations

Then split accounts into:

  • design partners
  • lighthouse logos
  • distribution partners
  • fast-closing revenue accounts

These are not the same thing.

3. Discovery

The goal of discovery is not to present.

The goal is to surface:

  • economic pain
  • operational blocker
  • compliance blocker
  • timeline pressure
  • internal champion
  • decision chain

Strong discovery questions:

  • What workflow today is slow, manual, or expensive?
  • Which part of settlement, onboarding, or reconciliation breaks most often?
  • What internal approval would block a new rail?
  • If this worked, where would you measure the savings?
  • Who signs if legal and compliance say yes?

4. Deal architecture

Most web3 deals fail because the commercial motion is not designed tightly enough.

For each opportunity, define:

  • legal structure
  • role of each party
  • custody and fund flow
  • compliance responsibilities
  • success metrics
  • pilot scope
  • expansion triggers

A pilot without expansion logic is often theater.

5. Post-close expansion

The first contract is not the finish line.

Good post-close BD asks:

  • What second workflow can be sold after the first win?
  • Can volume commitments or corridor expansion be added?
  • Can you become a platform dependency instead of a single feature vendor?

Pipeline management

Track deals by risk, not by optimism.

Every deal should have a clear red flag list:

  • no executive sponsor
  • unclear legal posture
  • no bankable fund flow
  • low pain intensity
  • long custom integration with weak revenue

If two or more red flags remain unresolved, downgrade the deal.

Metrics that matter

  • qualified pipeline by segment
  • time from first meeting to technical validation
  • time from technical validation to legal review
  • conversion by partner type
  • average implementation burden
  • net revenue retention or expansion rate

Working with product and legal

Weak BD throws a request over the wall.

Strong BD translates the account into crisp constraints:

  • target customer
  • exact workflow
  • minimum viable feature set
  • legal unknowns
  • operational dependencies
  • commercial upside

Partnerships

Not every partner is valuable.

A good partner has at least one of:

  • distribution
  • regulatory cover
  • liquidity
  • banking access
  • implementation leverage
  • credibility with your buyer

If a partner has none of those, it is mostly logo theater.

30-60-90 day operator plan

flowchart LR
    subgraph D1["Days 1–30: Orient"]
        A1[Map market\n& segment buyers]
        A2[Build target\naccount list]
        A3[Learn regulatory\nbasics]
        A4[Refine value\nproposition]
    end
    subgraph D2["Days 31–60: Qualify"]
        B1[Run structured\ndiscovery]
        B2[Identify pilot\nuse case]
        B3[Build internal\nplaybooks]
        B4[Qualify out\nweak deals]
    end
    subgraph D3["Days 61–90: Execute"]
        C1[Push to executable\npilot design]
        C2[Document objections\n& responses]
        C3[Build weekly\nreporting cadence]
    end
    D1 --> D2 --> D3

First 30 days

  • map the market and segment the buyer types
  • build a target account list
  • learn the core regulatory questions
  • refine the value proposition into plain business language

Next 30 days

  • run structured discovery
  • identify one strong pilot use case
  • build internal playbooks with legal and product
  • qualify out weak opportunities

Last 30 days

  • push one or two opportunities to executable pilot design
  • document objections and create response assets
  • build a repeatable reporting cadence for management

Rule of thumb

A strong BD operator is part salesperson, part strategist, part systems designer.

If you only do outreach, you are underpowered.

Chapter 07

12-Week Study Curriculum

A detailed study guide with weekly outputs and learning goals.

12-Week Study Curriculum

How to study

Study in three modes every week:

  • concept mode: understand the market and frameworks
  • operating mode: turn concepts into account strategy and deal logic
  • output mode: write, model, or present something so the knowledge becomes usable

You should produce artifacts weekly.

That is how you convert knowledge into career leverage.

12-week overview

flowchart LR
    subgraph Ph1["Phase 1: Foundation  Weeks 1–4"]
        W1[Wk 1\nIndustry Map]
        W2[Wk 2\nStablecoin\nEconomics]
        W3[Wk 3\nPayments\nMarket]
        W4[Wk 4\nTokenization\n& STO]
    end
    subgraph Ph2["Phase 2: Technical  Weeks 5–8"]
        W5[Wk 5\nRegulation]
        W6[Wk 6\nCustody &\nControls]
        W7[Wk 7\nCompliance\nStack]
        W8[Wk 8\nKorea-Led\nThesis]
    end
    subgraph Ph3["Phase 3: Execution  Weeks 9–12"]
        W9[Wk 9\nBD Discovery]
        W10[Wk 10\nDeal Design]
        W11[Wk 11\nThought\nLeadership]
        W12[Wk 12\nCareer\nPackaging]
    end
    Ph1 --> Ph2 --> Ph3

Weekly cadence

  • Monday: market reading and note consolidation
  • Tuesday: one deep concept topic
  • Wednesday: one company or protocol case study
  • Thursday: one account strategy or business model teardown
  • Friday: one memo or LinkedIn-quality note
  • Saturday: discussion, review, or language practice for client-facing communication

Week 1: Industry map

Learn:

  • market structure of crypto and digital assets
  • who the major buyer types are
  • why stablecoins, tokenization, and compliance matter now

Deliverable:

  • a one-page market map with your top three opportunity areas

Week 2: Stablecoin economics

Learn:

  • how stablecoin settlement works
  • corridor economics
  • float, fees, spreads, and treasury value
  • why enterprises care

Deliverable:

  • a memo comparing traditional cross-border settlement with a stablecoin-based workflow

Week 3: Payments market structure

Learn:

  • issuers, PSPs, acquirers, banks, wallets, and settlement providers
  • what part of the stack your target company can realistically own

Deliverable:

  • a market map of potential partners and competitors in one payment corridor

Week 4: Tokenization and STO

Learn:

  • issuance logic
  • servicing
  • custody
  • transfer restrictions
  • why tokenization often fails commercially

Deliverable:

  • a two-page concept note for one Korean tokenization use case

Week 5: Regulation fundamentals

Learn:

  • Korea, U.S., EU, Hong Kong, and Singapore at a high level
  • licensing perimeter
  • AML and travel rule basics
  • securities risk

Deliverable:

  • a country comparison sheet with practical BD implications

Week 6: Custody and controls

Learn:

  • custody models
  • policy engines
  • wallet governance
  • operational risk

Deliverable:

  • a checklist for enterprise digital asset onboarding

Week 7: Compliance stack

Learn:

  • KYC and KYB
  • wallet screening
  • transaction monitoring
  • sanctions exposure
  • reporting workflows

Deliverable:

  • a partner evaluation sheet for a compliance vendor

Week 8: Korea-led thesis building

Learn:

  • Korea-specific opportunity sectors
  • banking realities
  • how to localize value proposition
  • which domestic advantages are exportable

Deliverable:

  • your Korea-outward thesis in one page

Week 9: BD discovery and account planning

Learn:

  • how to run discovery
  • how to qualify opportunities
  • who the internal champions are

Deliverable:

  • one target account brief with proposed discovery questions

Week 10: Deal design

Learn:

  • pilot scoping
  • legal and operational assumptions
  • pricing logic
  • expansion path

Deliverable:

  • a sample pilot proposal with success metrics

Week 11: Thought leadership

Learn:

  • how to write clear market notes
  • how to explain web3 to enterprise buyers in plain language
  • how to show judgment without hype

Deliverable:

  • one publishable article or long post

Week 12: Career packaging

Learn:

  • how to present your thesis
  • how to document wins and case studies
  • how to speak like an operator, not a commentator

Deliverable:

  • a BD portfolio packet with your market thesis, target sectors, and one case study

Detailed topic guide

If you want depth, organize your note system around these topic buckets.

A. Stablecoins

Know:

  • issuer models
  • reserve model basics
  • settlement flow
  • wallet and custody choices
  • on-off ramp design
  • corridor economics
  • merchant and treasury use cases

Be able to explain:

  • why stablecoins matter even to users who do not care about crypto
  • why bank integration and compliance are part of the product

B. Tokenization

Know:

  • legal wrapper logic
  • difference between a token and a security claim
  • transfer restrictions
  • servicing and reporting
  • secondary trading challenges

Be able to explain:

  • when tokenization creates actual value and when it is mostly cosmetic

C. Compliance and regulation

Know:

  • KYC and KYB basics
  • travel rule
  • sanctions and wallet screening
  • licensing perimeter
  • securities risk

Be able to explain:

  • how regulatory uncertainty changes sales cycle design

D. Payments and treasury

Know:

  • settlement timing
  • liquidity needs
  • pre-funding
  • FX spread
  • reconciliation
  • treasury workflow

Be able to explain:

  • where the actual savings come from in a cross-border workflow

E. Enterprise sales

Know:

  • procurement logic
  • security review
  • legal review
  • executive sponsorship
  • pilot design

Be able to explain:

  • why enterprise deals fail and how to de-risk them early

Your reading discipline

Do not just consume crypto media.

Read across:

  • official regulator releases
  • bank and payment company announcements
  • issuer and custody documentation
  • serious market structure analysis
  • earnings calls and investor letters from adjacent fintech companies

The rule

Every week, convert knowledge into one of:

  • memo
  • presentation
  • account brief
  • market map
  • pitch rewrite

Otherwise the learning will remain passive.

Chapter 08

Scorecards And Templates

Reusable frameworks for partner qualification and account planning.

Scorecards And Templates

Opportunity scorecard

graph TD
    subgraph Factors["Score each factor 1–5  max total = 35"]
        F1[Pain Intensity]
        F2[Budget Owner Clarity]
        F3[Regulatory Feasibility]
        F4[Bankability]
        F5[Implementation Burden]
        F6[Expansion Potential]
        F7[Strategic Reference Value]
    end
    F1 & F2 & F3 & F4 & F5 & F6 & F7 --> T{Total Score}
    T -->|"28 or above"| GO[Strong Priority]
    T -->|"22 to 27"| MID[Keep in Motion]
    T -->|"21 or below"| SKIP[Likely Distraction]

Score each opportunity from 1 to 5 on:

  • pain intensity
  • budget owner clarity
  • regulatory feasibility
  • bankability
  • implementation burden
  • expansion potential
  • strategic reference value

Interpretation:

  • 28 and above: strong priority
  • 22 to 27: keep in motion but control scope
  • 21 and below: likely distraction unless strategically necessary

Partner scorecard

Ask:

  • Do they bring distribution?
  • Do they reduce legal or regulatory friction?
  • Do they improve liquidity or bank access?
  • Do they accelerate implementation?
  • Do they increase trust with the end buyer?

If the answer is "no" to most of these, the partnership is weak.

Discovery template

Use this structure in calls:

  1. Current workflow
  2. Current pain and where it shows up financially
  3. Existing workaround
  4. Internal stakeholders
  5. Compliance and operational blockers
  6. Timing and decision trigger
  7. Next step with a clearly owned action

Pilot design template

Define:

  • customer segment
  • use case
  • geography or corridor
  • fund flow
  • compliance responsibility
  • exact KPI targets
  • success condition for expansion
  • timeline and resourcing

Objection handling framework

Common objections:

  • legal is not comfortable
  • treasury does not want crypto exposure
  • integration looks heavy
  • there is no urgent need right now
  • a bank partner may reject it

Response pattern:

  • clarify the specific blocker
  • reduce scope
  • shift from ideology to economics and controls
  • introduce a regulated or trusted partner where relevant
  • create an option structure, not one all-or-nothing proposal

Account plan template

For each top target, write:

  • why this account now
  • what pain is most likely
  • who likely owns the budget
  • who blocks the deal
  • what reference would matter to them
  • what the smallest credible pilot is
  • what the second sale would be after the first

Meeting note template

  • objective
  • what was learned
  • what remains unclear
  • blockers
  • political map
  • next action
  • owner
  • deadline

Weekly BD review

Review these every week:

  • new pipeline created
  • qualified pipeline value
  • deals advanced
  • deals downgraded or killed
  • blocker themes
  • product asks created by real demand
  • legal or operational gaps slowing closure

Personal scorecard

Each month, score yourself from 1 to 5 on:

  • market knowledge
  • regulatory fluency
  • quality of discovery
  • deal design quality
  • executive communication
  • internal cross-functional leadership
  • writing and thought leadership

You improve faster when you can see where you are actually weak.

Chapter 09

Career Positioning

How to position yourself for senior BD and strategic roles.

Career Positioning

The role you are building toward

If you want to become a strong senior BD operator, head of BD, or strategic partnerships lead in web3, your profile should combine four things:

  • market judgment
  • commercial execution
  • regulatory awareness
  • narrative clarity

Most candidates have one or two. Few have all four.

How to stand out

1. Build a clear market thesis

Be known for a point of view, not for generic interest in blockchain.

A good thesis sounds like:

  • "Stablecoin-enabled treasury and cross-border settlement will be the strongest enterprise adoption wedge in Asia."
  • "Korea can export tokenization infrastructure if products are designed around regulated financial distribution."

2. Speak in business language

Executives do not need more crypto jargon.

They need:

  • revenue logic
  • cost logic
  • risk logic
  • implementation logic

If you can translate web3 into those terms, you become more valuable immediately.

3. Publish useful thinking

Write short memos or public posts about:

  • market shifts
  • regulatory implications
  • company strategy teardowns
  • Korea versus global comparisons

This compounds credibility and sharpens your own thinking.

4. Collect operating proof

Track your real work:

  • accounts opened
  • pilots designed
  • partnerships signed
  • corridor launches
  • process improvements
  • revenue influenced

Outcomes beat vague networking claims.

Your personal moat

For a Korea-based operator, a strong personal moat could be:

  • Korea fluency plus global market understanding
  • enterprise sales discipline plus crypto-native context
  • payments and tokenization knowledge plus partnership execution

That combination is rare enough to matter.

Suggested career assets

Build these:

  • one market thesis memo
  • one Korea-outward opportunity paper
  • one sample account plan
  • one sample pilot design
  • one list of market observations updated monthly

How to talk in interviews or senior meetings

Use this structure:

  1. Here is the market shift.
  2. Here is where the buyer pain is real.
  3. Here is the business model that captures value.
  4. Here is the operating risk.
  5. Here is how I would build pipeline and close motion.

That sounds like an operator.

What to avoid

  • vague claims about "community growth" without revenue or strategic impact
  • overuse of token price narratives
  • confusing partner logos with actual distribution
  • talking about web3 adoption without specifying workflow and buyer

Long-term goal

Over the next two to three years, aim to become the person who can do all of the following:

  • read a market shift clearly
  • identify which account types it creates
  • frame the legal and operating constraints
  • design a credible commercial motion
  • communicate it in board-safe language

That is a durable senior BD profile.

Chapter 10

Sources And Ongoing Reading

A source map and reading stack anchored to current official references.

Sources And Ongoing Reading

How to use this chapter

This is not meant to be a complete bibliography.

It is a practical source map for staying sharp. Start with official and primary sources whenever possible.

Current anchor references

Stablecoins and payments

https://www.whitehouse.gov/fact-sheets/2025/07/fact-sheet-president-donald-j-trump-signs-genius-act-into-law/

https://corporate.visa.com/en/sites/visa-perspectives/newsroom/visa-launches-stablecoin-settlement-in-the-united-states.html

https://www.circle.com/pressroom/circle-announces-payments-network-to-transform-global-money-movement

  • The White House fact sheet on the GENIUS Act signed on July 18, 2025:
  • Visa on stablecoin settlement in the United States and annualized volume context:
  • Circle announcement for the Circle Payments Network:

Europe and Asia regulation

https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/european-crypto-assets-regulation-mica.html

https://www.hkma.gov.hk/eng/news-and-media/press-releases/2025/07/20250729-4/

  • European Union MiCA summary and application timing:
  • Hong Kong Monetary Authority on the stablecoin ordinance coming into effect on August 1, 2025:

Korea policy and market structure

https://www.fsc.go.kr/eng/pr010101/82683

https://www.fsc.go.kr/eng/pr010101/84004

https://www.fsc.go.kr/eng/pr020101/83340

https://www.fsc.go.kr/eng/pr010101/86076

  • FSC on the Virtual Asset User Protection Act that took effect on July 19, 2024:
  • FSC on phased allowance of corporate participation in virtual asset transactions on February 13, 2025:
  • FSC on digital currency test participation and deposit token issuance sandbox on November 6, 2024:
  • FSC on the legal basis for security tokens as of January 15, 2026:

Market tracking

https://app.rwa.xyz/

  • RWA market dashboard:

Ongoing reading stack

Every week, scan:

  • FSC and KoFIU releases
  • Bank of Korea releases on digital currency and tokenization experiments
  • White House, Treasury, and major U.S. regulator updates where relevant
  • EU and MiCA implementation updates
  • HKMA and MAS releases
  • Circle, Visa, Stripe, Coinbase, Fireblocks, Chainalysis, and major custodians or infra providers

How to build your own source hierarchy

Use this order:

  1. Official regulator or government source
  2. Official company documentation or press release
  3. Primary market data source
  4. Serious research report
  5. Everything else

Your monthly review ritual

Once a month, answer:

  • Which jurisdiction became more open or more restrictive?
  • Which product category gained clearer commercial traction?
  • Which new buyer type appeared?
  • Which of my previous assumptions no longer looks right?

That is how you keep this book alive instead of static.
